original_abstract:
During the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4), we utilized high-resolution proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometry (PTR-TOF-MS) to characterize gas-phase emissions from a wide array of globally significant biomass fuels, including peat, crop residues, and various cooking fires. By integrating gas standard calibrations with composition-specific, mass-dependent transmission curves, we were able to quantify a complex mixture of non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs). The high-resolution capabilities of the TOF-MS allowed us to assign precise molecular formulas to major "exact masses" across a broad mass range. When combined with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, our methods successfully identified—positively or tentatively—between 80% and 96% of the total detected NMOC mass for key fuel types, a significant improvement over previous unit-mass resolution studies.

We provide detailed emission factors (EFs) for several compound classes that are often difficult to measure, such as furans, phenolic compounds, and aromatic hydrocarbons, many of which are important precursors for secondary organic aerosol (SOA) and ozone. Our results reveal that oxygenated NMOCs are the dominant emission category across all biomass fuels. We also compared traditional and advanced cooking methods, finding that open "three-stone" fires produced substantially higher EFs than cleaner-burning stove technologies. Additionally, we detected various little-studied nitrogen-containing organic species; while these accounted for only 0.1–8.7% of the fuel nitrogen, they may significantly influence new particle formation and regional nitrogen cycling. This comprehensive dataset offers a more complete accounting of fire-derived precursors, providing essential inputs for atmospheric models to better predict the chemical and climatic impacts of biomass burning.
humanized_abstract:
Data used in the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4) were analyzed with the aid of a high-resolution proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometer (PTR-TOF-MS). The fuel diversity studied here comprised several globally significant biomass fuels: peat, crop residues, and a variety of cooking fires. Concentration calibrations for known gas standards and mass-dependent transmission curves of composition-specific ions allowed for quantitative assessment of the non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) emitted from these diverse fuels. Exact molecular formulas were assigned to major peaks corresponding to major exact masses determined by the high-resolution time-of-flight (TOF) sector of the instrument. With the aid of Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, between 80% and 96% of the NMOC mass for each of the major fuel types was identified as being positive or at least tentatively assigned, a major improvement relative to previous unit-mass resolution analyses.

Here we present detailed emission factors (EFs) for a wide range of poorly characterised compounds such as furans, phenolics and aromatic hydrocarbons, many of the major SOA and O3 precursors. We show that oxygenated NMOCs are the largest emission category for all biomass fuels. We also investigate differences between traditional and advanced cooking methods and find large differences between the EFs for open three-stone fires and cleaner stoves such as improved cookstoves and Pellado Stoves. We also report on a wide range of lesser characterised nitrogen containing organic compounds, the majority of which are in the volatile fractions and constitute between 0.1-8.7% of the fuel nitrogen. Despite their small fractional contribution, they may be important in the new particle formation processes and may also have implications for the regional nitrogen cycle. The dataset presented in this paper gives a more complete picture of the emission of fire products and so the precursors that can be input into models in order to improve our knowledge of the impact of biomass burning on the atmosphere and climate.

document.input:
During the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4), we utilized high-resolution proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometry (PTR-TOF-MS) to characterize gas-phase emissions from a wide array of globally significant biomass fuels, including peat, crop residues, and various cooking fires.
